name: Oba
description: Oba is a prominent Orisha (deity) in Yoruba religion, venerated as the Orisha of the Oba River, which is located in Nigeria. She is recognized as one of the wives of Shango, the thunder god, and is often portrayed as a strong and fiercely independent warrior. Oba is associated with themes of loyalty, sacrifice, and the challenges faced by women in patriarchal societies. Her story is a cautionary tale about deception and the importance of self-preservation. Accounts depict her willingness to go to great lengths to earn Shango's love and attention, leading to a tragic outcome where she cuts off her ear and serves it to him as a meal, believing it would enhance her beauty and appeal. This act, motivated by jealousy and misguided advice from Oshun, instead repulses Shango. Oba is a symbol of female strength, resilience, and the enduring power of love, even in the face of betrayal and hardship. She is also a patron of marriage and domestic issues, and is often invoked to resolve conflicts and promote harmony within families. Her devotees seek her guidance in matters of love, relationships, and personal empowerment. Despite her tragic story, Oba remains a powerful and respected figure in Yoruba pantheon, representing the complexities of human emotions and the strength found in vulnerability.
subtitle: The Warrior Orisha of the Oba River

keyMyths.title: Oba's Ear
keyMyths.description: Oba, desiring Shango's love, was tricked by Oshun into cutting off her ear to feed him. This act backfired, repulsing Shango and leading to their separation.

keyMyths.title: The Rivalry with Oshun and Oya
keyMyths.description: Oba often found herself in competition with Oshun and Oya for Shango's affection, leading to strife and conflict within his household.

keyMyths.title: Oba's River
keyMyths.description: Oba transformed into the Oba River, a testament to her enduring power and connection to the natural world.
